perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this Agreement “ Confidential Information ” means all information concerning or provided by the disclosing Party (“ Disclosing Party ”) to the receiving Party (“ Receiving Party ”) or its Representatives (whether in writing, or in oral, graphic, electronic or any other form and including information made available or furnished prior to the date hereof) that is reasonably understood by the Receiving Party from the context of disclosure or from the information itself, to be confidential, and any report, analysis, compilation, study, interpretation, forecast, model, interpretation, third-party agreements or materials, trade secrets, customer and supplier information, product information, product roadmaps, records, memoranda or other material prepared or maintained by the Receiving Party, in whatever form (whether documentary, computer storage or otherwise) to the extent containing, reflecting, derived from, based upon or referring to, in whole or in part, any such information.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this Agreement “ Confidential Information ” means all information concerning or provided by the disclosing Party (“ Disclosing Party ”) to the receiving Party (“ Receiving Party ”) or its Representatives (whether in writing, or in oral, graphic, electronic or any other form and including information made available or furnished prior to the date hereof) that the Disclosing Party deems confidential, regardless of the Receiving Party's understanding or context of disclosure. This includes any report, analysis, compilation, study, interpretation, forecast, model, interpretation, third-party agreements or materials, trade secrets, customer and supplier information, product information, product roadmaps, records, memoranda or other material prepared or maintained by the Receiving Party, in whatever form (whether documentary, computer storage or otherwise) to the extent containing, reflecting, derived from, based upon or referring to, in whole or in part, any such information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines confidential information based on a reasonable understanding of confidentiality by the receiving party. The modified text makes it solely dependent on the disclosing party's discretion, irrespective of the receiving party's understanding, which contradicts standard definitions of confidential information and creates enforceability issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts the Trade Secrets Act by defining 'Confidential Information' solely based on the disclosing party's subjective determination. This conflicts with the Act's focus on reasonable measures to protect secrecy and information actually deriving independent economic value from not being generally known, readily ascertainable through proper means.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Confidential Information' to depend solely on the disclosing party's determination, overriding the receiving party's understanding. This contradicts the essence of trade secret protection, which, as implied by the provided snippet from 18 USC 1839, hinges on information deriving independent economic value from not being readily ascertainable, suggesting a standard of objective secrecy, not just subjective designation by one party. The change effectively nullifies the requirement that the information is reasonably understood as confidential by the receiving party, thus undermining the enforceability of the confidentiality clause and potentially conflicting with trade secret law principles.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and it will advise its Representatives who are informed as to the matters which are the subject of this Agreement, that Confidential Information may include material non-public information and that United States securities laws impose restrictions on trading securities when in possession of such information and on communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to trade in such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and it will advise its Representatives who are informed as to the matters which are the subject of this Agreement, that Confidential Information may include material non-public information and that United States securities laws impose restrictions on trading securities only when such information is explicitly designated as 'insider information' by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text acknowledges the standard restrictions on trading securities based on material non-public information. The modified text adds a requirement that the information be explicitly designated as 'insider information' by the disclosing party, creating a loophole that could allow trading on material non-public information if it is not specifically labeled as such.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 by suggesting that restrictions on trading securities only apply to information explicitly labeled as 'insider information.' This is incorrect because liability arises from trading on material, non-public information regardless of whether it's specifically designated as such by the disclosing party.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition that material non-public information must be explicitly designated as 'insider information' to trigger securities law restrictions, which contradicts the established legal understanding that trading on such information is restricted regardless of explicit labeling. Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, as explained, do not require such explicit designation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 78j, prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in securities trading, broadly encompassing the use of material non-public information for trading purposes, without mandating specific labeling of the information.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth herein, in the event that either Party or any of its Representatives is required (by law, regulation, court order or legal process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information or any of the information which is subject to the provisions of Section 2(d) above, such Party will provide the other Party with prompt written notice of such requirement prior to disclosure so that such Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. In the event that such protective order or other remedy is not obtained within the time limit of the requested or legally required disclosure, the Party compelled to disclose Confidential Information will furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information or take only such action as is requested or legally required based upon the advice of its legal counsel and will use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain reliable 2 assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded any Confidential Information (or other information required to be kept confidential pursuant to this Agreement) so furnished.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth herein, in the event that either Party or any of its Representatives is required (by law, regulation, court order or legal process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information or any of the information which is subject to the provisions of Section 2(d) above, such Party will provide the other Party with notification of such requirement within 30 days after disclosure. In the event that a protective order or other remedy is requested, the Party compelled to disclose Confidential Information will furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information or take only such action as is requested or legally required based upon its own discretion and will not be responsible for obtaining assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded any Confidential Information (or other information required to be kept confidential pursuant to this Agreement) so furnished.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires prompt written notice before disclosure and commercially reasonable efforts to obtain assurance of confidential treatment. The modified text changes the notice to after disclosure, removes the 'prompt' requirement, and shifts discretion from legal counsel advice to the party's own judgement. Furthermore, the responsibility to get confidential treatment is removed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: This change potentially contradicts FRCP Rule 26(c) which outlines protective orders. By delaying notice and removing the responsibility for obtaining assurance of confidential treatment, the modified text undermines the ability of the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order effectively, which Rule 26(c) aims to facilitate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the intent of FRCP Rule 26(c), as explained by the LLM, which seeks to facilitate protective orders. The original contract mandates 'prompt written notice' prior to disclosure to allow the other party to seek a protective order. The changed text alters this to notification 'within 30 days after disclosure,' which significantly undermines the opportunity to obtain a protective order before confidential information is released. Furthermore, the removal of the 'commercially reasonable efforts to obtain reliable assurance' clause and the shift of discretion to the party's 'own discretion' rather than legal counsel advice weakens the protection of confidential information. Although the snippets do not explicitly state rules about confidential treatment or protective orders, they generally govern discovery, and the Law Explanation asserts this change contradicts FRCP Rule 26(c). Therefore, given the provided information, the modifications introduce a legal contradiction by hindering the process of seeking a protective order.

perturbation.original_text: For a period commencing on the date of this Agreement and ending one (1) year thereafter (the “ Specified Period ”), each Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment any “Qualifying Person,” provided, however, that this section will not prevent either Party from: (a) engaging in discussions with a Qualifying Person where s/he has contacted such Party in response to (i) any general advertisement, job posting or similar notice; or (ii) an unsolicited resume or request for information from a Qualifying Person; or (b) engaging any recruiting firm or similar organization to identify or solicit persons for employment on behalf of such Party, or soliciting the employment of any specified officer or employee of a Party who is identified by any such recruiting firm or organization, in each case as long as such recruiting firm or organization does not directly target any officers or employees of a Party “ Qualifying Person ” shall mean any person who is an officer or employee of the other Party, who was introduced in person, by phone or email to the Party or its affiliates during the Specified Period in connection with evaluating a potential Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period commencing on the date of this Agreement and ending one (1) year thereafter (the “ Specified Period ”), each Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment any “Qualifying Person,” provided, however, that this section will not prevent either Party from: (a) engaging in discussions with a Qualifying Person where s/he has contacted such Party in response to (i) any general advertisement, job posting or similar notice; or (ii) an unsolicited resume or request for information from a Qualifying Person; or (b) engaging any recruiting firm or similar organization to identify or solicit persons for employment on behalf of such Party, or soliciting the employment of any specified officer or employee of a Party who is identified by any such recruiting firm or organization, in each case as long as such recruiting firm or organization does not directly target any officers or employees of a Party. “ Qualifying Person ” shall mean any person who is an officer or employee of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits the definition of 'Qualifying Person' to those introduced in connection with the potential transaction. The modification broadens the definition to include *any* officer or employee of the other party, regardless of their connection to the transaction. This severely restricts employee mobility and could be seen as an unreasonable restraint on trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: By broadening the definition of "Qualifying Person," the modified text creates a non-solicitation clause that effectively prevents the hiring of *any* employee of the other party for a year. This likely violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of "Qualifying Person" from those introduced in connection with a potential transaction to any employee of the other party. This significantly restricts employee mobility. The Law Explanation suggests this violates California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified text, by broadly prohibiting the solicitation of any employee, arguably restrains those employees from pursuing their profession, creating a direct conflict with BPC 16600(a).
